title: The post that drowned delivery
tagline: Ingress peaked at 140 rps. Your delivery workers died at 5,600. Someone forgot the ×40.
brief: You own notification delivery at a social app. Posts hit the Notify API, a fanout service expands each post into one delivery job per follower — 40 on average — and delivery workers write the notifications to Postgres. At t=20s a celebrity posts and ingress steps from 40 to 140 posts/s for fifteen seconds. The front door barely notices. The delivery tier detonates.
symptom: Watch the fanout's card when the spike lands: 140 rps in, 5,600 msgs/s out. The Delivery Workers pin at 100% CPU, log 'Dropping 57% of requests', and crash mid-spike — while the Notify API coasts at 18% utilization. Ingress never exceeded 140 rps; the flood is manufactured downstream.

phases.description: Celebrity posts — ingress steps to 140 posts/s
Back to baseline — the damage is done

accessPatterns.pattern: insert one row per follower per post

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Write-amplified notification store: rows written = posts × followers.

apiContracts.description: Create a post (triggers follower notification fanout)

diagnosis.question: During the spike the Delivery Workers pin at 100% CPU, log 'Dropping 57% of requests', and crash — while the Notify API sits at 18% utilization. What's the root cause?

options.text: Fan-out amplification: the delivery tier was sized for ingress, but every post becomes ~40 delivery jobs — 140 × 40 = 5,600 jobs/s against 2,400/s of worker capacity.
options.explain: Right. 12 workers × (1000 ÷ 5 ms) = 2,400 jobs/s of capacity, and the spike manufactures 5,600 jobs/s — 2.3× over, so the workers shed 57% and pin at 100% CPU until one crashes. Every number downstream of a fanout must be budgeted at ingress × multiplier, never at ingress.

options.text: The Notify API tier is under-provisioned and queues up posts during the spike, which back up into the rest of the pipeline.
options.explain: Its card disagrees: 4 instances × 200 rps = 800 rps of capacity against a 140 rps peak is 18% utilization, with zero errors and a flat p99 for the whole run. The number that explodes lives downstream of the fanout, not in front of it.

options.text: Postgres is write-saturated by the amplified inserts and backpressures the workers, which stalls delivery.
options.explain: Postgres never sees the flood: the workers forward only what they don't drop (~2,400/s) against 8,000 rps of combined DB throughput — about 30% utilization and zero errors all run. And the 'Dropping 57% of requests' line is logged BY the workers about their own saturation; drops originate at the worker tier, not below it.

options.text: A 3.5× traffic step is simply too violent — you need rate limiting at the edge to shed the spike before it enters the system.
options.explain: 140 rps is a trivial front-door load — the API tier absorbs it at 18% utilization, so edge rate limiting would throw away legitimate posts to protect a tier that isn't struggling. The violence is manufactured by the ×40 multiplier you control; the amplified burst should be absorbed downstream, not refused at ingress.

fix.objective: Survive the celebrity spike: notifications may land a few seconds late, but nothing crashes and the delivery workers drop nothing at any point in the run.
hints: Read the fanout's card during the spike: 140 rps in, 5,600 msgs/s out. The workers' capacity is 12 × (1000 ÷ 5 ms) = 2,400/s. The overload lasts 15 seconds — do you need 2.3× the fleet forever, or a place for 15 seconds of jobs to wait?

A queue between the fanout and the workers turns the spike into a backlog. Its drain is (groups × consumers ÷ processing ms) × 1000. Keep drain UNDER the workers' 2,400/s so they never drown, and ABOVE the 1,600/s baseline so the backlog shrinks once the spike passes.

Delete the Follower Fanout → Delivery Workers wire, add a Message Queue (1 group × 10 consumers × 5 ms ⇒ 2,000/s drain), and wire Fanout → Queue → Workers. Peak backlog ≈ (5,600 − 2,000) × 15 ≈ 54,000 messages, so give maxDepth comfortable headroom — 250,000.
